title: Dire straits: No way out of Hormuz
gist: Six months into the US-Iran war, the June ceasefire has collapsed after Trump reinstated the naval blockade and a vessel-escort fee, and the author argues Iran's existential framing of the conflict and retained strike capability mean Hormuz transit will keep slowing, oil prices stay pressured, and the crisis could still get worse rather than having peaked.

insights.claim: The Trump administration misread how the Iranian regime perceives the conflict: after its initial decapitation strategy failed, Washington offered a Memorandum of Understanding pushing the nuclear issue down the road and unfreezing billions in Iranian funds, believing this would be enough to convince Iran to let oil flows through Hormuz resume as normal.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: Identifies the specific negotiating misjudgment (treating an existential conflict as a transactional one) that caused the ceasefire's collapse, relevant for handicapping whether future US-Iran negotiations will hold.
insights.locator: The reasons why lie in how the United States

insights.claim: From Iran's perspective, the conflict is existential and the Islamic Republic has spent decades preparing for it; the status quo ante was never an acceptable resolution, since Iran is determined to emerge as the de facto manager of the Strait of Hormuz, making US attempts to pressure tankers toward the alternative Omani route a direct challenge to what Iran views as its rightful prize.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: Reframes the core disagreement as a fight over control of Hormuz itself, not just nuclear or sanctions terms — implying compromise proposals that don't address strait governance are unlikely to stick.
insights.locator: From Iran's perspective, however, this conflict

insights.claim: President Trump's latest move to reinstate the naval blockade and require a (subsequently scrapped) 20 percent payment for guarding escorted vessels is described as the final blow to the June ceasefire, signaling the White House has few remaining ideas to meaningfully change the conflict's dynamics.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: Marks a concrete escalation event (blockade reinstatement) investors should treat as confirmation the ceasefire is over and transit disruption will resume.
insights.locator: The latest announcement from President Donald Trump

insights.claim: The lack of clarity around Hormuz's future as a free waterway was an especially glaring omission in the Memorandum of Understanding, which enabled the Iranian regime's claims of ownership over the strait.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: Pinpoints a specific drafting gap in the collapsed deal, useful for evaluating whether any future negotiated settlement adequately addresses the core dispute.
insights.locator: The lack of clarity around Hormuz's future

themes.theme: Asymmetric endurance and Iran's retained capability

insights.claim: The Iranians have a stronger case than the US to reassure themselves of their own endurance, since the United States never maintains a presence anywhere in the Middle East forever and cannot run naval escorts or blockades in perpetuity.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: Challenges the assumption that sustained US military pressure will eventually force Iranian capitulation — time asymmetry favors Iran, a key input for duration-of-crisis forecasting.
insights.locator: Moreover, while both sides believe they can outlast

insights.claim: The ceasefire period likely enabled Iranian rearmament and preparation for renewed conflict; per the Institute for the Study of War, recent US strikes have attempted to degrade Iran's ability to attack commercial shipping but it remains unclear when that capability will be sufficiently degraded to prevent individual disruptive attacks.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
entities: Institute for the Study of War
insights.so_what: Suggests Iran's shipping-attack capability is durable despite months of US strikes, implying continued elevated shipping-disruption risk and insurance/freight cost premiums in the Gulf.
insights.quote: Recent US strikes have attempted to degrade Iran's ability to attack commercial shipping, but it remains unclear when Iran's ability to attack commercial shipping will be degraded sufficiently to prevent individual attacks that disrupt shipping.
insights.locator: As the latest analysis from the Institute for the Study of War

insights.claim: The Brent crude benchmark rose 10 percent just since the prior Sunday as transit through Hormuz, which had only mildly recovered during the brief ceasefire, is set to slow back to a crawl.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: A concrete, immediate price signal confirming the ceasefire collapse is already being priced into oil markets — directly actionable for energy/commodity positioning.
insights.locator: Oil prices are under pressure again

insights.claim: There are signs Yemen's Houthis, with Iranian support, are being activated for the first time in this conflict following recent claimed attacks on Saudi infrastructure; if they undermine transit in the Bab el Mandeb Strait, another four million barrels per day of oil transit could come under threat.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: Flags a second, largely separate transit chokepoint (Bab el Mandeb, ~4 million bpd) as an emerging escalation vector beyond Hormuz that could compound the oil-market shock if activated.
insights.locator: there are signs that the Yemeni Houthis

insights.claim: The latest International Energy Agency analysis asserts that the prospect of a supply surplus next year could be pushed further away if the crisis lingers, and the author warns that widely cited fears of oil surging above $150 per barrel at the war's start, which did not occur, should not be read as evidence the worst has already passed.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: Directly rebuts market complacency: the absence of a $150/barrel spike so far is not proof the tail risk has passed, and a delayed 2027 supply surplus should be factored into oil price forecasts.
insights.quote: Much commentary has been made of how the most dire prognostications at the start of the war—that oil could surge over $150 per barrel—did not occur. But that commentary has assumed the worst was past. In fact, the worst may be yet to come.

insights.claim: Regional oil producers are accelerating efforts to build expensive Hormuz workarounds, including multiple new pipelines to crisscross the Arabian Gulf, which the author reads as confirmation producers do not believe a durable solution to the strait crisis is on the horizon.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: New Hormuz-bypass pipeline construction is a concrete, trackable capex response and a signal of producers' own multi-year risk assessment — relevant for infrastructure/energy investors positioning around Gulf transit risk.
insights.locator: The accelerating efforts to plan and build

top_entities: Institute for the Study of War
